claimed_contribution: The paper identifies culture (individualism vs collectivism) as a determinant of fairness reactions to across-customer price differences.
It shows moderators of this fairness response, including salient self-construal and the comparison referent (friend vs stranger; loyal vs first-time buyer).

It provides process evidence through self-construal manipulation and face-related emotion (especially shame), and argues that prior price fairness work had not examined these cross-cultural mechanisms.
context: The studies examine consumer reactions to dynamic pricing when identical products are sold at different prices to different customers. The empirical contexts compare China and the United States and vary whether the comparison involves an in-group or out-group other (friend/stranger or loyal/first-time relationship).
design_and_data: The paper reports four scenario-based, between-subjects experiments. Study 1 (N = 334; Chinese and U.S. undergraduates) manipulated whether the participant paid a higher or lower price than a friend or stranger for the same item. Study 2 (N = 188; U.S. undergraduates) primed independent vs interdependent self-construal before similar price-comparison manipulations and measured fairness and repurchase intention; Study 3 (N = 270; China/U.S.) added an equal-price condition and measured emotions including shame and anger. Study 4 (N = 255; China/U.S.) operationalized in-group/out-group via buyer-seller relationship status (loyal vs first-time vs unspecified) and again measured fairness and repurchase; Chinese materials were translated and back-translated.
main_findings: Across studies, paying more than another customer reduced fairness judgments, and collectivist Chinese consumers showed stronger sensitivity to in-group versus out-group comparison targets than individualist U.S. consumers.

Within a single U.S. sample, priming interdependent (vs independent) self-construal reproduced the collectivist pattern of greater in-group sensitivity, and perceived fairness mediated effects on repurchase intention.

In the China-U.S. comparison, shame showed the predicted in-group-sensitive pattern among Chinese participants and mediated Chinese repurchase responses; the core cultural effect also replicated when in-group/out-group was defined by loyal versus first-time buyer-seller relationships.
one_line_summary: Across four experiments, the paper shows that collectivist Chinese consumers are more sensitive than individualist U.S. consumers to in-group versus out-group across-customer price differences, with face-related shame helping explain fairness and repurchase reactions.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior work on price fairness in across-customer pricing by adding a cross-cultural mechanism based on individualism/collectivism, self-construal, and face-related emotion. challenges fairness-and-culture accounts from reward-allocation settings that emphasize harmony or duty by showing a face-based, egocentric response pattern in pricing contexts.
research_gap: The paper states that although price fairness research exists, there is little empirical work on fairness reactions to across-customer price differences in a cross-cultural setting. It also notes that face has received scant attention in consumer research and was essentially unexamined for price perceptions.
research_question: How do consumers in collectivist versus individualist cultures judge the fairness of paying a different price than another customer for the same product? Does the in-group/out-group nature of the comparison and face-related emotion explain these cultural differences, and do they affect repurchase intentions?
